title-group.article-title: Serological Survey of Antibodies Against PPR Virus in Camels (Camelus dromedarius) in Khorasan Razavi Province, Iran

aff.named-content: Department of Microbiology and Molecular Genetics

aff.institution: Central Veterinary Laboratory, Iran Veterinary Organization

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Peste des petits ruminants (PPR) is a disease with high morbidity and mortality in small ruminants. A clinical report of this disease in camels has been made in Iran; however, no accurate information is available about infection with this virus in the camel population of Iran.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This study was designed and conducted to detect any trace of seroconversion in dromedary camels in Iran.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A total of 100 serum samples were collected from a slaughterhouse in Khorasan Razavi Province and analysed using a competitive enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay to detect antibodies against the PPR virus.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The results of this study indicated that the frequency of PPR virus exposure in camels in Khorasan Razavi Province was 3% (3 positive cases out of 100 camels). All the positive cases were female.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The seroconversion without any history of clinical disease among these camels indicated their exposure to the PPR virus in this region and their resistance to clinical disease. The resistance and sensitivity to this disease in camels have yet not been fully determined and require further studies. However, stressful risk factors such as transportation may contribute to the occurrence of clinical forms of PPR in camels. The detection of antibodies against the PPR virus in camels in this study reveals the fact that camels may be considerable contributors to the epidemiology of PPR in Iran.

abstract.p: This study was designed to detect any traces of infection or exposure to the PPR virus by serological method in camels in Iran. The results of this study indicated that the frequency of PPR virus infection in camels in Khorasan Razavi Province was 3%. The seroconversion without any history of clinical disease among these camels indicated their exposure to the PPR virus in this region and their resistance to clinical disease.

fn.p: Funding: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Peste des petits ruminants (PPR) is a disease with high morbidity and mortality in small ruminants. PPR virus, the causative agent of PPR disease, belongs to the genus Morbillivirus in the family Paramyxoviridae (Gibbs et al. 1979; Amarasinghe et al. 2019; Pope et al. 2013). Although this disease occurs mostly in sheep and goats, its clinical occurrence in camels has been reported in several countries, including Iran (Zakian et al. 2016), Sudan (Khalafalla et al. 2010) and Kenya (Omani et al. 2019). Experimental infection with the PPR virus has been reported in camels, large ruminants and some unusual hosts (Rahman et al. 2020). In addition, in several reports, seroconversion against the PPR virus in camels has been observed (Chemweno et al. 2019; Intisar et al. 2017; Woma et al. 2015). Some studies have also shown the existence of the PPR virus in camels by molecular methods (Khalafalla et al. 2010; Kwiatek et al. 2011). The PPR virus strains are classified into four different lineages (I, II, III and IV) based on the partial sequences of the nucleoprotein and the fusion protein genes (Balamurugan et al. 2010; Kwiatek et al. 2011; Munir et al. 2012; Tounkara et al. 2018). Sequencing of the PPR virus genome isolated from camels in Iran showed 99% similarity with the Iranian sub‐strain belonging to lineage IV (Zakian et al. 2016).

Clinical signs described in camels with PPR include yellowish and bloody diarrhoea, sudden death of apparently healthy animals (Khalafalla et al. 2010; Zakian et al. 2016), stomatitis, ulcerative keratitis conjunctivitis, fever, respiratory distress, pneumonia, enlargement of lymph node, dermatitis, obvious dehydration (Zakian et al. 2016), abortion, subcutaneous oedema, submandibular enlargement, chest pain and infrequent coughing (Khalafalla et al. 2010). However, an experimental study showed that dromedary camels are not sensitive to PPR after infection by a highly virulent isolate of PPRV (Fakri et al. 2019). Infected animals in this experimental study did not manifest any clinical symptoms of the disease (Fakri et al. 2019). In addition, some studies have reported seropositive camels without clinical signs (Chemweno et al. 2019; Woma et al. 2015; Swai et al. 2011).

The population of camels in Iran is about 151,804 (FAO 2020). Camels are usually kept in the pasture and have contact with sheep and goats. They are used not only as draft and riding animals but also for meat, milk, hide and wool. Camels are widely scattered in the great deserts in the central and eastern regions of Iran.

Low seroprevalence in the studies mentioned above (< 3%) compared to the high seroprevalence in small ruminants (up to 48.2% recently reported in India; Hota et al. 2018) and the lack of clinical signs in the experimental study (Fakri et al. 2019) may be an indication that camels are not sensitive to PPR. However, in Iran, a clinical form of PPR has been reported in camels (Zakian et al. 2016), with unavailable accurate information about the prevalence and frequency of infection with this virus in the camel population, so we conducted this study to evaluate the exposure of camels to the PPR virus by measuring the antibodies against the PPR virus and to determine the trace of infection with PPR virus in camels.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: This study was conducted on 100 camels of both sexes (14 males vs. 86 females) and different ages (1–15 years old) in a slaughterhouse in Khorasan Razavi Province. The camels were selected at random over 3 weeks. Simple random sampling with the ‘RANDBETWEEN’ function in Excel software version 2016 was used to select 100 camels from 172 camels; there was no restriction for selection. Eight animals, on average, were brought to the slaughterhouse a day, and among them, five animals were randomly selected per day. In total, 100 camels were selected from 172 animals. Due to financial constraints, we could take only 100 samples for analysis. These selected animals were from 20 herds, and most of them were from the east of Khorasan Razavi Province (Figure 1). Most of the population of camels are reared in small herds in the east of the province. Blood samples were collected from camels without anticoagulants. Then, these samples were centrifuged at 3000 RPM for 10 min. Sera were separated and stored at −20°C for further work.

caption.p: The distribution of the 20 herds (source of 100 selected camels) was shown in the map. Three exposed herds to the PPR virus (source of three seropositive camels) were characterised by a red colour.

sec.p: In this study, we used commercial ID Screen PPR Competition–IDVet kits (PPRC‐10P) to detect anti‐PPRV antibodies. The ELISA technique was performed as previously described (Libeau et al. 1995) and according to the kit's instructions.
SPSS version 26 was used for data analysis, and the chi‐square tests were used for comparing the groups, and p < 0.05 was considered significant.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The results of the serological survey for the PPR virus in camels of different ages and both sexes are shown in Table 1. The frequency of serological response against PPR virus exposure in camels was 3% (3 camels out of 100 camels). The distribution of the 20 herds (source of 100 selected camels) was shown on the map. Three exposed herds to PPR virus (source of 3 seropositive camels) were characterised by red colour (Figure 1). All camels with antibodies against the PPR virus were female, although there was no statistically significant correlation between different sexes exposed to the PPR virus (p > 0.05). In addition, there was no statistical correlation among different age groups exposed to the PPR virus in camels (p > 0.05).

caption.p: Frequency of positive camels for PPR virus antibodies according to age and sex of camels in Iran.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, the frequency of PPR virus exposure in camels was 3%. Other studies conducted in Saudi Arabia (2.92%) (Hemida and Al‐Ghadeer 2019), Kenya (3%) (Chemweno et al. 2019), Sudan (2.1%) (Intisar et al. 2017), Nigeria (3.36%) and some other countries have also reported a similar seroprevalence for PPR virus in camels. Since the clinical form of PPR in camels has occurred in our country (Zakian et al. 2016), it is expected that the prevalence and frequency of antibodies against the PPR virus will be high; however, this study showed a low frequency of exposure to the PPR virus in studied camels in Khorasan Razavi Province. The low frequency of antibodies against the PPR virus in camels may be due to their resistance to this disease. This finding is supported by an experimental study conducted with the intravenous administration of a virulent PPR virus strain in camels (Fakri et al. 2019). No clinical symptoms were found in this experimental study. In our study, seropositive camels had no history of clinical form of PPR, and there was no history of PPR in their herds either. The same findings have been reported in several other studies (Woma et al. 2015; Swai et al. 2011; Hemida and Al‐Ghadeer 2019). In a review study, it has been stated that stress, such as environmental change, may impair the general condition of the camels and cause the clinical form of PPR (Rahman et al. 2020). In the study reporting the clinical form of PPR in camels in Iran (Zakian et al. 2016), the camels had been imported from Kuwait 2 weeks before the onset of clinical disease. The fact that these camels were not native to Iran and were transported before the occurrence of the disease may have contributed to the occurrence of the clinical form of PPR in these animals. However, this conclusion requires further study to confirm the effectiveness of risk factors on the occurrence of the clinical form of PPR in camels. Nevertheless, the presence of antibodies against the PPR virus in camels in our study, although low frequency, indicated exposure of these animals to the PPR virus. This finding suggests that camels may play a role in the epidemiology of the PPR virus in Iran.

Some studies have reported the prevalence of PPR in sheep and cattle in Iran, for instance, Rasooli et al. (2018) reported a seroprevalence of PPRV of 58% in sheep and 23% in cattle and Allahvirdizadeh, Houshangi, and Eshratkhah (2020) reported a seroprevalence of 28% in sheep. The seroprevalence reported in these animals was much higher than the frequency reported in camels in our study (3%). In addition, in the outbreaks of PPR that occurred in Iran in 2006 (Abdollahpour et al. 2006), there was no report about the occurrence of the clinical form of PPR in camels. To explain these findings, it is fair to say that camels may be less susceptible to PPR than sheep and goats. In general, seroconversion against PPRV in camels should be considered important and considered in the eradication programs of PPR, and more studies should be done in this field.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Camels in Iran have been exposed to the PPR virus, and there are antibodies against this virus in their serum, so camels are considerable contributors to the epidemiology of the PPR virus in Iran.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Afshin Raoofi: conceptualisation, project administration, supervision. Omid Madadgar: methodology, visualisation. Abdolbasir Aghebatinia: data curation, investigation. Reza Hassanzadeh: formal analysis, methodology. Arman Shokri: writing–original draft.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: Approval was obtained from the Local Ethics Committee of Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tehran (3486).

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.70121.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
